posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Just got mine. Only had time to flip through it a bit, but it appears that all the photos are unlabeled? Here’s a picture of a vineyard, or someone in a winery, but we’re not going to tell you which one/who? Really? Or am I missing something? Is the photo ID buried in the text somewhere?

Substance looks great but this (and the flamingo-pink spine) are odd missteps to make in a book this comprehensive, expensive, and presumably excellent otherwise.

posts.name: MikeBasile
posts.cooked: I agree on the photos. Would have been nice to have a label for quick reference.

posts.name: Brady Daniels
posts.cooked:
I’m still not 100% sure what these books profess to be. What will we learn?

Amazon says…

From Burgundy to Bordeaux and everywhere in between, this is sure to be the resource on modern French wine for decades to come.

I suspect it would mischaracterize the books to say this is a pretty old-school way of looking at France, assuming they focus on the two B’s, whilst mentioning a few other growers along the wine road.

For those who receive them soon, I’d really love to know what you learn from these books. Are they a broad intro to the wines of France, or do they really focus on what’s changing? Are they an encyclopedia of knowledge, or do they focus on what’s been worthy / great, and what is becoming great, and explain why he thinks so?

Thanks.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
There are two volumes. The second volume is more like an encylopedia, with a list of producers organized by region and then a few paragraphs on each. These are a selection, certainly not an attempt to list every producer in any given regions. The regions go far beyond Bdx and Burg. These listings seem to have a few sentences on the house style or approach and then a sentence or two each about different bottlings from that producer. No discussion of vintages.

The first volume I haven’t spent as much time with yet, but it seems to be more a narrative look at the French wine industry today and the changes it has seen in recent years.

posts.name: Ben Herd
posts.cooked: Anyone spent more time with this book since the release? It was $70 on Amazon today but still wonder about the content quality.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: A very impressive book project. Bonné sees France reemerging to a new future following a difficult century that started with phylloxera. He weaves a grand story - this is a book with a red thread - of renewal and strife that for the most is succesful. The usual elements are also here: terroir, viticultural improvements, new vinification techniques, but he places it all in a historical context taken fra 19th century French mapping of its wine legacy. Thoroughly recommended, and gives food for thought also when you think you are in the know. Book 1, a lenghy narrative, is far better than book 2, producer profiles.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Very curious on the content of this one.

Currently reading through The Sommelier’s Atlas of Taste which is a fun and enjoyable read. One thing about highlighting a select few producers from a given region as favorites or top quality is it can perpetuate the hype cycle already driven by the internet, modern critics, and social media. As an example there might be a given producer I really like, already collect or have been meaning to try, and as a result of the hype cycle prices and availability is awful and only getting worse. Would be nice for these books to highlight undervalued and lesser known producers. Or producer who have recently improved, rather than at times feeling like a replay of all the labels I see all over social media.

I started a thread a little while back on the hype-cycle of Arnoux-Lachaux and how prices have gotten silly. I don’t really need another report form William Kelley (whom I adore BTW), on how great the AL wines are. I’d much rather time spent on attainable wines. Names that might have been unfashionable a decade ago, but with improvements and stylistic changes are making great wine now and thus they’re actually obtainable.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

The usual elements are also here: terroir, viticultural improvements, new vinification techniques, but he places it all in a historical context taken fra 19th century French mapping of its wine legacy.

Let me ask you this. In discussing appellations, communes, or even crus, to what extent does he answer these questions:

  1. Why did this wine become prized and what were the taste characteristics then?
  2. What are the characteristics of the best examples, why are they best, how have they changed through the years, and is that change for the better or worse?
  3. How do the wines of this place differ from similar wines in other places?

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: If you are looking for buying advice, keep up subscriptions. Books like Bonné’s do indirectly give them, but through broader take on wine regions, not through points.

reply_to_user.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: Yes and no. Yes, because I think you do to some extent find such answers. No, this is not that kind of book. Taste characteristics is but a small part. So you have to look for yourself as I find your questions coming from a somewhat surprising angle.

posts.name: Dave Hendrik
posts.cooked: I picked it up, too. Enjoyable to skim so far. I think it will also be really useful when trip planning and seeking out interesting producers.

posts.name: Stuart Martin
posts.cooked:
What I appreciated most about the producers volume was that it focused on the next generation of winemakers that will shape French wine for the next 25+ years. I thought this was refreshing since most of the wine books you see focus on the benchmark producers. I enjoyed the Sommelier Atlas of Taste, but 90% of the producers covered are far beyond most people’s budgets. Certainly informative, but not exactly helpful in terms of buying.

Given the interest in finding and tasting wine ahead of the curve, I thought the producers section was right up this forum’s alley.

For example, I bought the book about 6 weeks ago, and it was incredibly helpful in helping me identify winemakers for potential tastings on our recent trip to Burgundy, some of whom are not widely discussed on this forum. As an aside, Maison Harbour from Berserker Day received an excellent write-up.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: The author had a podcast on the Wine Enthusiast channel last month where he discussed the book and the research process . it’s an okay listen. Apparently it took him 8 years to write this.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: I think much less of the producers’ profiles than the analysis in part 1. Jon Bonné’s take on French wine over the last two centuries is a major intellectual achiement. This is a lasting contribution rising way above his wine writing peers. By all means enjoy his producer portaits (I think he is less impressive here), but take your time to access his arguments and understanding in part 1.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: Let me add this: Even if you can find faults and disagreement, and get irritated with talkative passages and some self-centeredness, I find it learned and thought provoking. Most interesting chapter: Languedoc. Least interesting chapter: Bordeaux.

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked: Does he discuss the impact of Maison Ilan?

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Well, Arnoux-Lachaux was actually unfashionable a decade ago. My predecessors in TWA didn’t review them. Thing move fast…

Of course! But is it too much to ask that you also dive deep on today’s obtainable producers?

In all seriousness one of my biggest recent surprises was a wine from Michel Mallard, and some of your reviews tipped me over the edge on buying some. Availability and pricing are good, and I think they even have some slightly older vintages available. Prime example of a producer where a deep dive is more fun and useful than AL in today’s market. But naturally, give it a year and I’ll just be complaining about how I can’t get any and prices have gone up.
